title: Acrylic Paint on Cotton
stainChemistry: Acrylic paint contains acrylic polymer emulsion, pigment, and water. While wet, it is water-soluble. Once dry, the acrylic polymer cross-links into a flexible, water-resistant film on cotton. This transition from water-soluble to water-resistant happens relatively quickly (15-30 minutes) and is irreversible. Dried acrylic paint is essentially a plastic coating.
whyThisWorks: If wet: immediate cold water flushing and surfactant removes acrylic paint easily from cotton — it is fully water-soluble before curing. If dry: isopropyl alcohol or amyl acetate softens and dissolves the cured acrylic film. Scraping the softened film with a dull edge helps mechanical removal. The window between 'easy' and 'very difficult' is narrow — treat immediately for best results.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply amyl acetate or appropriate solvent to dissolve dye/pigment matrix
2. Apply NSD to lift loosened pigment. Flush with cool water
3. If color persists: apply H2O2 6% on colorfast fabrics to break chromophore bonds
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
1. Blot immediately — never rub (spreading dye worsens it)
2. Apply Dawn dish soap mixed with cold water. Blot gently
3. If stain persists: apply hydrogen peroxide 3% on colorfast fabrics — test hidden area first, 5-10 min dwell time
5. Air dry — no heat until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional — they have specialized dye-stripping agents
warnings: Never rub dye stains — rubbing spreads the color

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If the dye has penetrated the fiber (no improvement after 3 solvent passes), or if the fabric is expensive/delicate, or if dye continues to spread during treatment
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is paint acrylic on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the color still actively spreading or has it dried?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
